title: Costs and Cost Behaviour
description: Quiz covering cost classifications, cost behaviour, fixed costs, variable costs and mixed costs.

questions.question: What is a business cost?
options: The value of resources consumed to produce goods or services
The amount of money customers pay for a product
The total value of company assets
questions.explanation: A cost represents the value of resources used to operate the business or produce products and services.

questions.question: Which of the following is typically a fixed cost?
questions.explanation: Rent normally remains unchanged regardless of production volume within the relevant range.

questions.question: Which statement best describes a variable cost?
options: It changes in proportion to production or sales volume
It only changes once per year
It is always higher than fixed costs
questions.explanation: Variable costs increase or decrease as the level of activity changes.

questions.question: Which of the following is most likely to be a variable cost in a manufacturing company?
questions.explanation: Raw materials are consumed as products are manufactured, making them a variable cost.

questions.question: What is a mixed (semi-variable) cost?
options: A cost containing both fixed and variable components
A cost that only occurs once
A cost paid entirely in cash
questions.explanation: Mixed costs combine a fixed element with a variable element.

questions.question: A company pays a fixed monthly internet subscription plus additional charges based on data usage. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: The fixed subscription fee and variable usage charges together form a mixed cost.

questions.question: What happens to total fixed costs when production increases within the relevant range?
questions.explanation: Within the relevant range, total fixed costs remain constant regardless of production volume.

questions.question: What happens to total variable costs as production increases?
options: They increase as more units are produced
questions.explanation: Total variable costs rise because more resources are consumed when producing more units.

questions.question: A company has fixed monthly costs of $12,000. If production doubles from 500 units to 1,000 units, what happens to the total fixed costs?
options: They depend entirely on sales revenue
questions.explanation: Total fixed costs remain unchanged within the relevant range.

questions.question: A company has variable costs of $9 per unit. How much are the total variable costs if 400 units are produced?
questions.explanation: Total Variable Cost = Variable Cost per Unit × Units Produced = $9 × 400 = $3,600.

questions.question: What is meant by the 'relevant range' in cost behaviour?
options: The range of activity where cost behaviour assumptions remain valid
The period during which a company earns a profit
The number of products a company can sell
The range of market prices for a product
questions.explanation: The relevant range is the level of activity where fixed and variable cost assumptions remain accurate.

questions.question: Why is the concept of the relevant range important?
options: Because fixed costs may change if production exceeds certain limits
Because revenue becomes fixed outside the relevant range
Because profit can only be calculated within the relevant range
questions.explanation: If production expands significantly, businesses may need additional facilities or equipment, causing fixed costs to increase.

questions.question: A factory currently rents one warehouse. If production doubles and the company must rent a second warehouse, what happens to the rent expense?
options: It increases in steps rather than remaining constant
It decreases because of economies of scale
questions.explanation: Fixed costs often increase in steps when the business exceeds the relevant range.

questions.question: Which formula is used to calculate total cost?
questions.explanation: Total cost equals total fixed costs plus total variable costs.

questions.question: A company has fixed costs of $25,000 and variable costs of $15 per unit. If 2,000 units are produced, what is the total cost?
Why do managers study cost behaviour before preparing a budget?
options: To estimate how costs will change as activity levels change
To determine the company's tax rate
questions.explanation: Understanding cost behaviour allows managers to prepare more realistic budgets.

questions.question: Which of the following costs is most likely to behave as a mixed cost in a manufacturing business?
options: Electricity with a fixed monthly fee plus charges based on usage
questions.explanation: Electricity often contains both a fixed connection charge and a variable usage charge.

questions.question: A shipping company notices that fuel expenses increase every time vessel activity increases. Fuel is therefore best classified as:
questions.explanation: Fuel consumption generally increases as vessels operate more voyages.

questions.question: Which statement about fixed costs is most accurate?
options: They remain constant in total within the relevant range but decrease per unit as production increases
They always increase proportionally with production
They remain constant both in total and per unit
questions.explanation: Total fixed costs stay constant, but when spread across more units, the fixed cost per unit decreases.

questions.question: A manager incorrectly assumes that all costs increase with production. What is the greatest risk of this assumption?
options: The manager may overestimate future costs and make poor business decisions
The company will automatically become unprofitable
Variable costs will become fixed costs
Revenue can no longer be calculated
questions.explanation: Understanding which costs actually change is essential for accurate forecasting and decision-making.

questions.question: A company expects demand to increase by 40% next year. Why is understanding cost behaviour important before increasing production?
options: It helps estimate how total costs and profitability will change
It guarantees that demand will increase
It determines the selling price automatically
questions.explanation: Managers use cost behaviour to forecast costs, profits and resource requirements as production changes.

questions.question: A manufacturing company doubles its production while remaining within its relevant range. Which statement is correct?
options: Total fixed costs remain the same, while total variable costs increase
Both fixed and variable costs remain unchanged
questions.explanation: Within the relevant range, fixed costs remain constant while variable costs increase with production.

questions.question: A company wants to reduce its average cost per unit without changing its fixed costs. Which strategy is most likely to achieve this?
options: Increase production volume within the relevant range
questions.explanation: Producing more units spreads fixed costs over a larger number of products, reducing the fixed cost per unit.

questions.question: A shipping company is evaluating two operating schedules. One schedule increases fuel costs but allows the vessels to transport significantly more cargo. Which type of cost should management pay the closest attention to in this decision?
questions.explanation: Fuel is a variable cost that changes with vessel activity and directly affects operating decisions.

questions.question: A business experiences a large increase in demand and must hire an additional production supervisor. What type of cost behaviour does this illustrate?
options: A fixed cost that never changes
questions.explanation: Some fixed costs increase in steps when production exceeds the relevant range.

questions.question: A manager assumes that electricity is entirely a variable cost, even though the company pays a fixed monthly connection fee. What is the problem with this assumption?
options: It ignores the fixed component of a mixed cost
Electricity is always a fixed cost
Electricity has no impact on profitability
questions.explanation: Many utility costs contain both fixed and variable elements, making them mixed costs.

questions.question: Which manager demonstrates the best understanding of cost behaviour?
options: A manager who distinguishes between fixed, variable and mixed costs before preparing budgets
A manager who assumes all costs change at the same rate
A manager who ignores cost classifications
A manager who bases decisions only on last year's profit
questions.explanation: Understanding how different costs behave leads to more accurate planning and better business decisions.

questions.question: Why is cost behaviour considered one of the foundations of managerial accounting?
options: Because many budgeting, pricing and investment decisions depend on predicting how costs change
Because it determines government tax policy
questions.explanation: Managers rely on cost behaviour to forecast expenses and evaluate business alternatives.

questions.question: A business incorrectly classifies several fixed costs as variable. Which consequence is most likely?
options: Forecasts, budgets and profitability analyses may become inaccurate
The company will eliminate all operating costs
questions.explanation: Incorrect cost classification can lead to poor planning and poor managerial decisions.

questions.question: What is the ultimate reason managers study cost behaviour?
options: To make better business decisions by understanding how costs respond to changes in activity
To maximize production regardless of demand
To ensure every cost becomes variable
questions.explanation: Understanding cost behaviour allows managers to budget more accurately, set prices, evaluate investments and improve profitability.
